_comment: Canonical VirtualSMS copy values. Generated-from: Design/positioning.md (tone) + Design/brand-numbers.md (digits). Repos and pages should GENERATE descriptions from this, never retype. Verified 2026-07-16; mcpToolsPublished note corrected 2026-07-17; supplier names PURGED 2026-07-17 (see notes.supplierNames). READ _guardLimit BEFORE TRUSTING A GREEN GUARD.
_noNames: THIS FILE CONTAINS NO SUPPLIER NAMES, BY RULE, AND MUST NOT ACQUIRE ANY. It is vendored byte-for-byte into 5 SDK repos plus mcp-server and ships inside 4 published packages, so any name written here is published to the world. If you are about to add a supplier name to enforce a ban: STOP. That is the exact defect the 2026-07-17 purge removed, and it is self-defeating, the anti-leak file becoming the leak. Ban the SHAPE ('our <x> fleet'), not the name. If the shape cannot be expressed without the name, declare it in guard.unenforceable[] with a reason, as two entries already do. See notes.supplierNames.
_guardLimit: A GREEN GUARD MEANS 'NO BANNED STRINGS'. IT DOES NOT MEAN 'ACCURATE'. scripts/check-positioning.sh bans LITERAL STRINGS from avoid[]: it catches '$0.02', 'Ranked #1', '12 tools', '35 tools' and em-dashes. It CANNOT catch a number that is true today and false tomorrow. '18 tools' is accurate right now and goes false the moment feature/mcp-tier-a-hardening merges, exactly as '12 tools' was true once and then survived for months on a 90.8k-star list. The guard would have passed every single day of that. Nothing the guard runs can verify a count; only re-deriving it from the published server's tools/list can (see notes.mcpToolsPublished and Decisions/2026-07-17-mcp-server-publish-spec.md sections 2g, 6, 7). The same caveat covers every moving target in numbers{}: services, numberCountries, proxyCountries, the price floors and successFraming are point-in-time readings with a verification date, NOT invariants. When the code moves, the copy is part of the move.
entitySentence: VirtualSMS is an account verification platform for individuals, developers, and AI agents. It combines one-time SMS verification, dedicated number rentals, matching-country proxies, and private cloud browser sessions behind one API, one MCP server, and one prepaid balance.
infrastructureLine: VirtualSMS provides the infrastructure behind reliable account verification workflows.
devTagline: Built for developers and AI agents: REST API, hosted MCP server, SDKs.
mantra: Real carrier SIMs. Matching-country IPs. Private browser. One balance.
brandIdea: your number, IP and browser tell the same story
precedence: Vault/CLAUDE.md and ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md carry the operator's ABSOLUTE rules. If anything in this file conflicts with them, CLAUDE.md WINS. This file is DERIVED (from Design/positioning.md + Design/brand-numbers.md) and never overrides an ABSOLUTE rule; it cannot create a new opsec ban on its own. Every avoid[] entry MUST have an avoidProvenance[] record. An entry with no provenance is UNVERIFIED and must NOT be enforced as an opsec rule until traced to an ABSOLUTE rule or a dated operator ruling: report it to the operator, do not enforce it and do not silently delete it. Test that resolves this class: does the phrase describe the SIM/number the CUSTOMER RECEIVES (product, allowed) or OUR hardware/fleet/location/counts (infrastructure, banned)? Reason: 2026-07-16, an unprovenanced 'physical SIM' ban drifted into the avoid-list, contradicted the ABSOLUTE opsec rule (which explicitly allows physical-SIM product wording), and an approved differentiator was scrubbed across 6 repos and 4 published packages.
avoid: <supplier> name in a browser-visible provider field

avoidProvenance.source: Vault/CLAUDE.md 'No infrastructure location / exact-fleet leak' (operator hard rule 2026-06-02); Decisions/2026-06-02-no-infra-location-leak-hard-rule.md
avoidProvenance.note: Also covers modem/SIM box/ports, our fleet/our hardware, any fleet-location statement, any exact SIM/port/modem count.

entries: <supplier> name in a browser-visible provider field
avoidProvenance.source: Vault/CLAUDE.md 'No provider / supplier-name leak' (operator hard rule 2026-06-12, REWRITTEN 2026-07-17); Decisions/2026-06-12-no-provider-name-leak-hard-rule.md. Carve-out: operator ruling 2026-07-02, Decisions/2026-07-02-1720-opsec-rules-product-vs-supply-clarification.md. Purge ruling: operator 2026-07-17, 'remove the providers from all the code and wording, we don't need provider names anywhere!! what's so hard about this?? make it a fucking rule.. so we dont even need to worry about this in the first place.'
avoidProvenance.note: READ THIS BEFORE ADDING A SUPPLIER RULE. These entries are DELIBERATELY WRITTEN WITH A <supplier> PLACEHOLDER AND NO REAL NAME. Until 2026-07-17 they spelled the names out in order to ban them, which made THIS FILE the largest concentration of supplier names in the whole estate: it is vendored byte-for-byte into 5 SDK repos plus mcp-server and ships inside 4 published packages, so the anti-leak file was the leak. It blocked a push on 2026-07-17 for containing the very strings it existed to remove. The operator's ruling is architectural and it is correct: if the name exists NOWHERE, there is nothing to guard and no rule to remember. THE RULE NOW: supplier names appear in no code, config, copy, identifier or comment in any repo. The ONLY permitted mentions are competitor context, Vault docs and Linear tickets. CARVE-OUT, UNCHANGED AND RULED TWICE (2026-07-02, 2026-07-16): our suppliers are ALSO public competitors. Comparison pages, vs-tables and 'Alternative to <them>' copy are ALLOWED and SEO-valuable; the alternative-to page is the best-performing page. NEVER scrub those. The purge targets the SUPPLY RELATIONSHIP, exactly as before; what changed is that the guard no longer needs the name to enforce it. Test, unchanged: does it reveal WHO SUPPLIES US? Server-side internals (server logs, DB columns, env vars, Vault docs, Linear tickets) remain out of scope for the browser-leak test, but per the 2026-07-17 ruling they are still purge targets wherever changing them is not a migration; where it IS a migration, report it and do not change it unilaterally.

avoidProvenance.source: Opsec half: Vault/CLAUDE.md infra-leak rule (2026-06-02). Tone half: Decisions/2026-07-16-positioning-aeo-copywriting-framework.md ('platform' is the customer noun; 'infrastructure' only describes depth).
avoidProvenance.note: NOT a blanket ban on the words. Opsec only when the phrase states WHAT or WHERE we operate. Apply the product-vs-infrastructure test.

avoidProvenance.source: Decisions/2026-04-29-1500-agents-subdomain-surgical-fix.md ('owns its modem fleet end-to-end - not a reseller' flagged FALSE: VirtualSMS is HYBRID, own SIMs + supplier aggregation). 'direct-sourced' was introduced 2026-07-16 as a replacement phrase (Operations/2026-07-16-sdk-positioning-review-B-mcp.md) and inherits the same false claim.
avoidProvenance.note: Banned because UNTRUE, not because it leaks. Do not escalate into an opsec sweep.

avoidProvenance.source: Decisions/2026-07-16-positioning-aeo-copywriting-framework.md, whose frontmatter scopes it 'canonical_for: all VirtualSMS marketing/product/blog page copy' and whose rule reads VERBATIM: "Wording: use 'AI agents', NOT 'agentic workflows' (search/recognition; 'agentic' only in deep docs/blog)." Scope narrowed from README+manifests to manifests-only on 2026-07-17 (expert review), on the authority of notes.agentTagline (operator call 2026-07-16).
avoidProvenance.note: SCOPE: PUBLISHED DESCRIPTIONS ONLY. Enforced by rule tone-agentic at include 'manifests'; READMEs and docs are deliberately OUT of scope. THREE grounds, all canon's own words: (1) the source decision is scoped BY ITS OWN FRONTMATTER to marketing/product/blog PAGE copy, and a developer repo README is documentation, not page copy; (2) that decision's own sentence carves docs out in terms ('agentic only in deep docs/blog'); (3) notes.agentTagline is a DATED OPERATOR CALL drawing this exact line: agent-first language is allowed in dev-repo README BODIES, while package.json/server.json/registry DESCRIPTIONS must lead with entitySentence. Note the decision bans the PHRASE 'agentic workflows' as a substitute for 'AI agents'; the bare token is merely the machine-checkable proxy for that inside a short description. HISTORY, and it is the point: until 2026-07-17 this rule scanned README.md, so canon turned CI RED on a word its own provenance expressly permits in docs. That is the notes.physicalSIM defect shape (a nuanced decision flattened into a broader ban than its provenance authorises), caught this time before it scrubbed anything. Do NOT re-widen to 'all' or 'customerFacing' without a NEW operator ruling that supersedes notes.agentTagline.

avoidProvenance.source: Decisions/2026-07-16-positioning-aeo-copywriting-framework.md guardrails: never 'identity' framing; copy is about the user's OWN accounts.

Operator + expert review ruling 2026-07-17 (locked in the 2026-07-17/18 session). 'Communications infrastructure' is Twilio's category; VirtualSMS solves the narrower account-verification wedge and must not adopt the broad Twilio framing.
avoidProvenance.note: BANS THE TWO-WORD PHRASE 'communications infrastructure' ONLY, never the bare word 'infrastructure'. The word 'infrastructure' is APPROVED in the sanctioned usages 'provides the infrastructure behind reliable account verification workflows' (about/dev surfaces, see infrastructureLine) and 'Infrastructure for AI agents that need real-world phone verification' (GitHub/MCP tagline). Rule tone-comms-infra is a literal substring match on the full phrase, so it cannot reach either approved line; both are pinned in guard.mustNotMatch.allProfiles so REFUSAL 2 proves it at sync time. PLATFORM-VS-INFRASTRUCTURE RULE (operator + expert 2026-07-17): 'platform' = what VirtualSMS IS (homepage/registry: 'account verification platform'); 'infrastructure' = what it ENABLES (about/dev: 'provides the infrastructure behind ...'). We are not saying VirtualSMS IS infrastructure, we are saying it PROVIDES infrastructure. See notes.platformVsInfrastructure.

avoidProvenance.source: Design/brand-numbers.md 'Format rules' sweep targets (last block added 2026-07-16). brand-numbers.md is authoritative for all digits.

Design/brand-numbers.md 'Format rules' -> 'Historical drift to clean up (sweep targets)': 2000+ services, 2000 services, 500 services, 2500 services (no-plus from the earlier policy), 700+ services, 200+ countries, 100+ countries, 180+ countries. '500+ services' is from the same file's 'Anti-drift rule' table, which records mcp-server src/resources.ts shipping '500+ services'. Added to avoid[] 2026-07-17.
avoidProvenance.note: THESE WERE ALREADY CANON AND ENFORCED NOTHING. brand-numbers.md is authoritative for all digits and this file is supposed to be the machine-readable copy it is generated into, but only 3 of its 8 sweep targets had ever been copied here, and check-positioning.sh hand-enforced a 4th ('500+ services') that was in NEITHER list. Three lists, three different contents, which is the hand-copy defect this file exists to stop, occurring inside the anti-drift files themselves. Verified zero hits across all 6 guarded repos on 2026-07-17, so adding them is a pure gap-close with no copy change. Format note: '2500 services' bans the NO-PLUS form; the canonical string is '2500+ services' and the numeric matcher's not-preceded-by-a-digit guard is what keeps '500 services' from firing on it.

avoidProvenance.source: Decisions/2026-07-17-mcp-server-publish-spec.md section 2h (2026-07-17). Recorded there and banned here because it had ALREADY leaked into this file's own notes.mcpToolsPublished and would otherwise return from any old transcript.
avoidProvenance.note: PHANTOM NUMBER: 35 was NEVER a real tool count on any branch. It was a stale session artifact, an MCP client's tool list loaded at session start before the dist was rebuilt 2026-07-16 22:12, reported once in a session and typed straight into canon. Real state: 18 published, 44 on feature/mcp-tier-a-hardening, 41 shipped by default. Banned as the PHRASE '35 tools', NOT the bare digit: 35 is legitimate elsewhere (rgba opacity 0.35 across Design/, faq.json item counts) and a bare-digit ban would be the flatten-a-nuanced-rule-into-a-blanket-ban failure this file exists to prevent (see notes.physicalSIM). KNOWN LIMIT, and it is exactly the point of _guardLimit: the original drift phrasing was 'a branch has 35', which this literal entry does NOT match. The guard cannot catch every phrasing of a wrong number, and it cannot catch a right number going stale. Only re-deriving from the published tools/list can.

avoidProvenance.source: Design/brand-numbers.md 'No AI-slop / no em-dashes' (operator hard rule 2026-07-15). Covers en-dash too; ranges use a plain hyphen.

guard._comment: MACHINE-READABLE ENFORCEMENT SPEC for scripts/check-positioning.sh. This block does NOT create bans. Every rule either lists the avoid[] entries it enforces in covers[], or cites in authority[] the avoidProvenance note that already authorised the expansion. It says HOW an existing ban is machine-checked, never WHAT is banned. Precedence is unchanged: CLAUDE.md wins, this file is DERIVED. Added 2026-07-17 to fix a structural defect: check-positioning.sh HARDCODED every banned string and cited this file only in a comment, so the guard was itself a hand-typed copy of canon that nobody re-derived, which is the exact defect it exists to catch, and adding an entry to avoid[] enforced NOTHING.
guard.howItWorks: Design/sync-canonical.js reads this block and writes two GENERATED artifacts into each guarded repo: scripts/canonical.json (a byte-for-byte copy of this file) and scripts/positioning-rules.txt (the rules below, flattened for the profile). check-positioning.sh is a dumb interpreter: it hardcodes ZERO brand strings and reads every pattern from the rules file. Never hand-edit either artifact; sync-canonical.js is the only sanctioned writer.
guard.theForcingFunction: The generator REFUSES to emit if any avoid[] entry is neither listed in some rule's covers[] nor declared in unenforceable[]. That failure is the entire point. An avoid[] entry must either enforce something or be VISIBLY declared unenforceable with a reason. It may never silently enforce nothing, which is the state this block was written to end.
guard.theIncidentControl: The generator ALSO refuses to emit if any rule matches any string in allowed[] or mustNotMatch. This is the machine version of the 2026-07-16 incident: a 'physical SIM' ban would match the approved differentiator 'real physical SIM cards' and the generator would now REFUSE TO BUILD instead of scrubbing it across 6 repos. A ban that flags approved copy is a bug in the ban, and it is caught at sync time, not by an operator noticing months later.

profiles.sdk: The 5 SDK repos (node, python, virtualsms-php, ruby, dotnet): 4 to 6 tracked files each, README plus a manifest, no competitor-comparison content. Re-verified 2026-07-17.
profiles.mixed-content: Repos carrying BOTH product copy and competitor-comparison content. Currently: mcp-server, whose README has a migrate-from-a-competitor section and whose package.json carries a competitor-alternative keyword. The website and frontend, if ever guarded, take THIS profile, never sdk.

profilesNote._warning: THIS NOTE LIVES OUTSIDE guard.profiles ON PURPOSE. sync-canonical.js iterates Object.keys(guard.profiles) and treats EVERY key as a profile name, so a documentation key added in there becomes a phantom profile with zero rules, and REFUSAL 1 then reports every avoid[] entry as enforcing nothing. That is not hypothetical: it happened while writing this note on 2026-07-17 and the generator caught it immediately, which is the forcing function working exactly as designed. Keep prose out of that object.
profilesNote._note: AS OF 2026-07-17 THE TWO PROFILES RESOLVE TO IDENTICAL RULE SETS, and that is a RESULT, not an oversight. The split existed for exactly one reason: flat supplier-NAME bans were safe in the name-free SDK repos but forbidden in mixed-content, where they would flag the comparison content the operator ruled ALLOWED twice. The purge ruling deleted every flat name ban, and a rule that contains no name cannot flag a bare name, so the hazard the split protected against no longer exists and every supplier rule is now safe everywhere. The machinery is deliberately KEPT rather than ripped out: it costs nothing, the targets already declare their profile, and the next genuinely profile-specific ban (surface scope, docs-vs-manifest tone) should not have to rebuild it. If you are adding a rule, default to both profiles and justify a split, not the reverse.

surfaces.customerFacing: README.md package.json setup.py pyproject.toml composer.json *.gemspec *.csproj *.nuspec server.json .plugin/plugin.json
surfaces._note: 'readme' is any-depth on purpose (preserves the pre-2026-07-17 dash scope). 'customerFacing' is the ROOT readme plus publish manifests: a bare pathspec is rooted, so it will not reach examples/*/README.md, which canon classes as docs. server.json and .plugin/plugin.json were added 2026-07-17: both are independently-set PUBLISH surfaces with a description field, both were previously policed by nothing. brand-numbers.md 'Where these numbers must match' already lists both.

rules.reason: canonical services count is 2500+

rules.label: 12 tools
rules.reason: canonical published MCP tool count is 18
rules.excludeReason: CHANGELOG.md is carved out for THIS RULE ONLY. mcp-server's CHANGELOG records '12 tools across discovery, account, and order-management categories' under '[1.0.0] - 2026-03-15 / Added'. That is TRUE as history: v1.0.0 did ship 12 tools. A Keep-a-Changelog entry is an immutable dated record of what a release contained, and the ban targets CURRENT claims (the failure was '12 tools' surviving on a live 90.8k-star listing, not in a changelog). Banning it here would force falsifying history to get a green check, which is worse than the drift. The carve-out is deliberately the smallest shape that fits the evidence: it does NOT extend to the class. num-35-tools keeps NO carve-out because 35 was never true in any tense, so no changelog can honestly record it.

rules.label: 35 tools
rules.reason: phantom count; it was never a real state of any branch. Published count is 18
authority: avoidProvenance '35 tools': ban the PHRASE '35 tools', never the bare digit 35, which is legitimate as 0.35 opacity and as item counts. The numeric matcher's not-preceded-by-a-digit guard also stops it firing on '135 tools'.

rules.label: 90%+ of VoIP
rules.reason: unsupported claim; canonical success framing is 95%+
canonical min price per code is $0.05

rules.label: near-100
rules.reason: canonical success framing is 95%+

rules.label: Ranked #1
rules.reason: unverifiable ranking claim

rules.label: #1 on
rules.reason: unverifiable ranking claim
authority: Expansion of avoid[] 'Ranked #1'. The string actually live on npm, server.json and the GitHub About blurb is '#1 on ChatGPT and Perplexity', which the literal 'Ranked #1' does not match. Decisions/2026-07-17-mcp-server-publish-spec.md section 3a.

rules.label: #1 in
rules.reason: unverifiable ranking claim
authority: Expansion of avoid[] 'Ranked #1', same shape as frame-1-on.

rules.label: not a reseller
rules.reason: untrue; VirtualSMS is HYBRID (own SIMs plus supplier aggregation)

rules.label: direct-sourced
rules.reason: untrue; inherits the 'not a reseller' false claim

rules.label: agentic
rules.reason: canonical wording in a published description is 'AI agents', not 'agentic'
rules.includeReason: MANIFESTS ONLY. README.md is deliberately EXCLUDED, narrowed 2026-07-17. The delta from the previous 'customerFacing' scope is exactly ONE path: README.md; every publish manifest stays banned, so no published DESCRIPTION loses cover. AUTHORITY: notes.agentTagline, an operator call of 2026-07-16 on precisely this axis, 'Per-surface agent-first tagline is allowed in dev-repo README BODIES, but package.json/server.json/registry DESCRIPTIONS must lead with entitySentence'. This rule is now the machine form of that sentence: banned in every published description, allowed in dev-repo README bodies and docs. See avoidProvenance 'agentic' for the two supporting grounds (the source decision's own frontmatter scope, and its own deep-docs/blog carve-out). COUNTER-ARGUMENT CONSIDERED AND REJECTED: 'the README is an SEO surface and the ban's stated reason is search/recognition, so it should stay banned there.' The source decision already weighed search/recognition and STILL allowed 'agentic' in the BLOG, which is unambiguously an SEO surface. SEO-ness is therefore not the line that decision drew; customer-facing page copy versus deep docs is, and a developer README sits on the docs side. WHY IT MATTERED, not hypothetical: the old scope named README.md, so writing 'agentic' in a developer README failed CI, which made canon block language the 2026-07-17 expert review recommends leaning into. RESIDUAL GAP, declared rather than hidden: 'manifests' does not reach website/frontend page copy (.tsx), which is where this ban actually bites. The frontend is not currently guarded. If it is ever guarded (profile mixed-content, per guard.profiles), a marketing-page surface MUST be added to guard.surfaces and named here, or this rule will silently enforce nothing on the one surface the source decision was actually written about.

rules.label: communications infrastructure
rules.reason: Twilio's category; VirtualSMS solves the narrower account-verification wedge, and 'platform' (not broad 'communications infrastructure') is the customer noun
authority: Operator + expert review ruling 2026-07-17. BANS THE TWO-WORD PHRASE ONLY. The bare word 'infrastructure' stays APPROVED for the sanctioned 'provides the infrastructure behind ...' and 'Infrastructure for AI agents ...' usages; a literal substring match on the full two-word phrase cannot reach either, and both are pinned in guard.mustNotMatch.allProfiles so REFUSAL 2 proves it at sync time. See avoidProvenance 'communications infrastructure' and notes.platformVsInfrastructure for the platform-vs-infrastructure rule.

rules.label: physical SIM infrastructure
rules.reason: infra leak; the SIM is the PRODUCT and is allowed, the infrastructure is not
authority: Was in avoid[] and enforced by NOTHING before 2026-07-17: the old guard's bare 'modem' ban catches 'physical SIM modems', 'hardware modems' and 'own modem fleet', but the string 'physical SIM infrastructure' contains no 'modem' and slipped through. Zero hits in all 6 repos at time of adding.

rules.label: modem
rules.reason: infra leak; never describe our hardware

rules.label: SIM box
rules.reason: infra leak; never describe our hardware
authority: avoidProvenance absolute-opsec note: 'Also covers modem/SIM box/ports, our fleet/our hardware, any fleet-location statement, any exact SIM/port/modem count.'

rules.label: our fleet
rules.reason: infra leak; never describe our fleet

rules.label: our hardware
rules.reason: infra leak; never describe our hardware

rules.label: our EU
rules.reason: infra leak; never reveal where our hardware sits

rules.label: ports across
rules.reason: infra leak; never reveal port or SIM counts
authority: avoidProvenance absolute-opsec note: 'any exact SIM/port/modem count'.

rules.label: SIM cards in <region>
rules.reason: infra leak; never say where our SIMs are
authority: avoidProvenance absolute-opsec note: 'any fleet-location statement'. Deliberately narrow. The trailing ([^a-zA-Z]|$) is load-bearing: without it the 'us' alternative fires on the legitimate phrase 'SIM cards in use'. Anchored on a location word next to OUR inventory, never on a bare region word and never on a country or carrier name.

rules.label: SIMs in <region>
rules.reason: infra leak; never say where our SIMs are

rules.label: <region> mobile networks
rules.reason: infra leak; never say where our SIMs are
authority: avoidProvenance absolute-opsec note: 'any fleet-location statement'. Carrier NAMES stay allowed: 'a German number runs on Telekom' must pass.

rules.label: our <inventory> are in <region>
rules.reason: infra leak; never say where our fleet is
authority: avoidProvenance absolute-opsec note: 'any fleet-location statement'. This is also the machine-checkable half of the 'we operate' / 'our network' mixed class: it matches the narrow LEAKING shape (stating what or where we operate) without banning the bare words. See unenforceable[].

rules.label: our <supplier> fleet
rules.reason: supplier or infra leak; the whole SHAPE is banned, whatever fills the slot
authority: NAME-FREE, both profiles, per the 2026-07-17 purge ruling. This works without a name because the ENTIRE SHAPE is banned, not just the name-filled version: 'our <anything> fleet' is a fleet claim by construction, so the slot filler is irrelevant and the rule needs no vocabulary of suppliers. It therefore also enforces the older ABSOLUTE infra rule that literal 'our fleet' (rule opsec-our-fleet) could never reach, because an inserted word defeats a substring match. Verified 2026-07-17: catches the historical leak 'our <supplier> fleet', and additionally catches three real infra leaks live in VirtualSMS-local that the previous name-based rule missed entirely ('our German fleet', a fleet-LOCATION leak, and 'our Spotify fleet' x2). Zero hits in all 6 guarded repos. Cannot flag the comparison carve-out: it contains no name, so no bare name can match it.

rules.label: our <supplier> integration
rules.reason: supplier leak; naming who we integrate with reveals who supplies us
authority: NAME-FREE, both profiles, per the 2026-07-17 purge ruling. Same construction as supplier-our-x-fleet: the shape is the ban. Verified 2026-07-17 to catch both historical integration leaks, 'our <supplier> integration' and the longer sentence it actually shipped in, 'live via our <supplier> integration'. Zero hits in the 6 guarded repos. Known true-positive it will fire on if the frontend is ever guarded: an admin settings page reads 'connects directly to the <competitor> API using our custom integration', which states a supply relationship and should be rewritten, not exempted.

rules.label: we source/buy/resell from
rules.reason: supplier leak; states the supply relationship in the active voice
authority: Expansion authorised by the avoidProvenance supplier note ('the purge targets the SUPPLY RELATIONSHIP'). Name-free by construction: it bans ADMITTING the relationship regardless of who the counterparty is, which is the active-voice half of the passive 'sourced from <supplier>' shape declared unenforceable below. Zero hits in all 6 guarded repos and in VirtualSMS-local, verified 2026-07-17, so this is a pure gap-close with no copy change.

rules.label: em-dash or en-dash
rules.reason: not allowed in README; use a colon, comma or full stop
authority: The avoid[] entry 'em-dash' is the NAME of a character, not text to grep: a literal grep for the string 'em-dash' would flag this file, positioning.md and every doc that discusses the rule, while matching zero actual em-dashes. It is enforced instead by this dedicated rule type, which matches the U+2014 and U+2013 CODEPOINTS. The interpreter builds both with printf so the script stays pure ASCII and encoding-safe. This is the one avoid[] entry whose enforcement is not a substring of itself.

rules.label: em-dash or en-dash
rules.reason: not allowed in a package manifest description; use a colon, comma or full stop

unenforceable.reason: NOT MACHINE-CHECKABLE NAME-FREE, and this was PROVEN, not assumed, before declaring it. The 2026-07-17 purge ruling removed supplier names from this file, so a rule may no longer contain one. Every name-free pattern that catches the historical leak 'numbers sourced from <supplier>' also flags APPROVED copy, which would trip REFUSAL 2 and, worse, would scrub live product copy exactly as the 2026-07-16 incident did. Measured against the real corpus: /sourced from/ destroys 6 approved lines, including 'Real SIM cards sourced from 145+ countries worldwide' and 'US numbers are sourced from real US carrier-issued SIMs' (both live product copy describing the PRODUCT, which the opsec rule expressly allows), plus the comparison-carve-out line 'numbers sourced from SMS-Activate's supplier pool'. Narrowing to /sourced from <word>/ changes nothing: same 6 casualties. Even the brand-shaped-suffix hack /sourced from [a-z0-9-]*(sms|verified|activate)/ still destroys the comparison line, and it is a name ban wearing a disguise, which is precisely what the ruling forbids. The ONLY token distinguishing the banned line from the approved ones IS the supplier's name. A guard forbidden to know the name therefore cannot make this distinction, and no amount of pattern cleverness changes that. COMPENSATING CONTROL, which is the whole point of the architecture: the name is purged from the codebase, so there is no name left to be sourced-from. The active-voice half IS machine-checked by supplier-we-source-from. The passive half is a human review item at copy review. Accepting a declared, visible gap beats shipping a rule that cries wolf, because a guard that cries wolf gets deleted.

entries: <supplier> name in a browser-visible provider field
unenforceable.reason: NOT MACHINE-CHECKABLE NAME-FREE. Tautological and worth stating plainly: detecting a name requires the name. The banned wire shape is a provider field carrying a supplier's name; the approved shape is the same field carrying the opaque token. The two are structurally identical and differ ONLY in the value, so the pattern that separates them must enumerate either the banned names (forbidden by the 2026-07-17 ruling) or the allowed values (which POSIX ERE cannot express as a negation: git grep -E has no lookahead, and grep -P is banned estate-wide because it errors on locale and falsely reports clean). Expressing it would mean adding a rule TYPE to check-positioning.sh, which is hand-maintained in 6 repos and is not generated: out of scope for a purge, and the wrong trade. COMPENSATING CONTROLS, both verified 2026-07-17: (1) the backend already emits the opaque token, /api/v1/rentals returns "provider":"network"; (2) the frontend no longer carries a supplier name to send. The residual risk is honest and stated: nothing in the guard will catch a NEW supplier name introduced into a provider field. That is the accepted cost of a name-free guard, and the mitigation is the rule itself, that the name never enters the codebase, not a grep that would have to hold the name to look for it.

Class mixed-opsec-tone. avoidProvenance says in terms: 'NOT a blanket ban on the words. Opsec only when the phrase states WHAT or WHERE we operate.' Whether a given use leaks depends on the sentence, and a substring matcher cannot read a sentence. A flat ban would flag 'we operate a REST API' and 'our network of supported services', and a guard that cries wolf gets deleted, which is worse than no guard. The narrow LEAKING shape IS machine-checked by rule opsec-our-inventory-in-region ('our SIMs/fleet/infrastructure are in ...'). The remainder is a human review item at copy review, not a grep.

Class decision-risk-framing, and a bare common noun. A flat ban is a false-positive machine: verified 2026-07-17 that it matches the MIME type 'application/vnd.groove-identity-message' inside bundled dependency code in mcp-server (.smithery/bundle.cjs, .smithery/shttp/module.js), and it would match 'identity verification' in any competitor description or third-party quote. The actual rule is about FRAMING (copy is about the user's OWN accounts, never someone's identity), which requires reading a sentence's meaning. Not machine-checkable as a substring. Human review item.

mustNotMatch._comment: APPROVED COPY THAT MUST NEVER MATCH ANY RULE. sync-canonical.js asserts this at generation time and REFUSES to emit on a hit. This is the standing, automated version of the manual control that used to be re-run by hand: it makes the 2026-07-16 incident structurally impossible to repeat, because the ban that would have scrubbed the differentiator cannot be built. allowed[] is appended to allProfiles automatically. Add a line here whenever an operator ruling approves a phrase.

VirtualSMS is an account verification platform for individuals, developers, and AI agents. It combines one-time SMS verification, dedicated number rentals, matching-country proxies, and private cloud browser sessions behind one API, one MCP server, and one prepaid balance.
VirtualSMS provides the infrastructure behind reliable account verification workflows.
Infrastructure for AI agents that need real-world phone verification.
mustNotMatch._supplyNote: The last 7 lines were added 2026-07-17 with the purge and they are load-bearing, not decoration. 'our global inventory' is the sanctioned replacement PR #498 used for the scrubbed fleet claim, and it is the fixture that PROVES this file does not ban 'our <x> inventory': that noun is deliberately absent from supplier-our-x-fleet's pattern precisely because banning it would destroy its own approved replacement. The two 'sourced from' lines are live product copy describing the PRODUCT (allowed by the opsec rule's own product-vs-infrastructure test) and they are the fixtures that would fail the build if anyone ever 'fixes' the declared 'sourced from <supplier>' gap with a flat ban. That gap is unenforceable ON PURPOSE; see unenforceable[]. These fixtures are how the file defends its own reasoning against a future good intention.
mustNotMatch._mixedNote: The mixed-content-only block was REMOVED 2026-07-17. It held competitor-context fixtures ('Alternative to <supplier>', '<supplier> alternative', the alternative-to comparison link) whose job was to prove the flat name bans could not reach a repo with comparison content. Two things changed and both point the same way. First, those fixtures SPELLED THE SUPPLIER NAMES OUT, so under the purge ruling they were themselves a leak, and a large one: this file is vendored into 6 repos and 4 published packages. Second, they now test NOTHING: no rule in this file contains a supplier name, so no rule can match a bare supplier name, and the property the fixtures asserted is guaranteed by CONSTRUCTION rather than by assertion. A fixture that cannot fail is not a test. The carve-out itself is UNCHANGED and still absolute: comparison and 'alternative to' content is ALLOWED and SEO-valuable, and must never be scrubbed.

allowed: real carrier-issued mobile numbers, not VoIP

notes.physicalSIM: ALLOWED product differentiator, operator ruling 2026-07-16. 'physical SIM' was REMOVED from avoid[] on that date: it had drifted into the avoid-list and contradicted the actual opsec rule, causing the differentiator to be scrubbed across 6 repos. The SIM is the PRODUCT (allowed); the modem and where it sits is our INFRASTRUCTURE (banned). Still banned: physical SIM infrastructure, physical SIM modems, hardware modems / modem / SIM box / ports, our fleet / our hardware / own modem fleet, any fleet-location claim, not a reseller / direct-sourced. Do NOT re-add 'physical SIM' to avoid[].
notes.mcpToolsPublished: DERIVED VALUE, NEVER TYPED. The tool count equals whatever the PUBLISHED server's tools/list returns. Read it, do not type it. Every wrong count in this file's history came from a human typing a number instead of reading one. PUBLISHED TODAY = 18: npm virtualsms-mcp@1.2.3 + hosted mcp.virtualsms.io tools/list + origin/main, all three cross-checked 2026-07-16. Claim 18. It is what ships. NOT YET PUBLISHED, do not claim: branch feature/mcp-tier-a-hardening is 44 total DEFINED, or 40 shipped by DEFAULT. Default 40 INCLUDES the cloud-browser start tool (ungated, beta). Setting VIRTUALSMS_ENABLE_SESSIONS adds the 3 session tools = 43; release_rental is gated behind VIRTUALSMS_ENABLE_RELEASE; both flags set = 44. Decomposition of the 26 new: 10 rentals + 10 proxy + 3 session (gated behind VIRTUALSMS_ENABLE_SESSIONS) + 3 other. Arithmetic: 18 + 26 = 44 total defined; default 40 = 44 minus 3 session minus 1 release_rental (gated behind VIRTUALSMS_ENABLE_RELEASE). Corrected 2026-07-17/18: the browser start tool ships ungated by default and release_rental joined the gated set, so DEFAULT is 40, not the earlier 41. Nothing from that branch is public (origin/main is still 4a6ac73, 1.2.3, 2026-04-29), so 44/41 are claims about someone's disk, not about the product. Do NOT claim them until pushed, version-bumped, published and re-verified against tools/list. CAUTIONARY, DO NOT REINTRODUCE: this note previously read 'A local unpushed branch has 35 (adds 10 rental + 4 proxy + 3 other)'. BOTH halves were wrong. 35 was a STALE SESSION ARTIFACT (an MCP client's tool list loaded at session start, before the dist was rebuilt 2026-07-16 22:12) and was NEVER a real state of any branch, and the '4 proxy' arithmetic was wrong (there are 10). If you find 35 in an old transcript, that is this artifact resurfacing: it is not evidence. Corrected 2026-07-17 per Decisions/2026-07-17-mcp-server-publish-spec.md section 2h. The lesson is the file's own: a number observed once, written down and never re-derived is a drift vector, and it does not matter that the file it lands in is the anti-drift file.
notes.supplierNames: OPERATOR HARD RULE, 2026-07-17: supplier names appear in NO code, NO config, NO copy, NO identifier and NO comment, in ANY repo. Use opaque tokens ('network'). The ONLY permitted mentions anywhere are (1) COMPETITOR CONTEXT, which is ALLOWED and SEO-valuable and must NEVER be scrubbed (our suppliers are also public competitors; the alternative-to page is the best-performing page, ruled twice, 2026-07-02 and 2026-07-16), and (2) Vault docs and Linear tickets, which are private. Server-side DB values and env vars where a change would be a migration: REPORT, do not change unilaterally. WHY THE RULE IS ARCHITECTURAL AND NOT JUST TIDINESS: a name that exists nowhere cannot leak, cannot drift into a diff, and needs no rule anyone has to remember at 2am. Every previous control here was a grep that had to HOLD the name in order to look for it, which is why this file, the anti-leak file, ended up the single largest concentration of supplier names in the estate and blocked a push on 2026-07-17 for containing the strings it existed to remove. Deleting the names deleted the problem class rather than policing it. WHAT THE GUARD CAN AND CANNOT DO NOW, stated honestly because a green check must never overstate itself (see _guardLimit): it CATCHES the supply-relationship SHAPES that survive without a name ('our <x> fleet', 'our <x> integration', 'we source from'), which is 3 of the 4 known historical leaks, and it catches strictly MORE real infra leaks than the old name-based rules did, because a shape ban does not care what fills the slot. It CANNOT catch a bare name: that is tautological, detecting a name requires the name, and two such shapes are declared in guard.unenforceable[] rather than faked. The compensating control is the rule itself, not a pattern.
notes.x402: Do NOT claim live. Endpoints 404 (VSMS-465); the discovery manifest is the artifact that is wrong. Use x402-ready or omit.
notes.agentTagline: Per-surface agent-first tagline is allowed in dev-repo README BODIES, but package.json/server.json/registry DESCRIPTIONS must lead with entitySentence (operator call 2026-07-16).
notes.entitySentence: REPLACED AGAIN 2026-07-18 (operator-approved, expert review pass, locked this session). The change from the 2026-07-17/18 version: the tail 'in one workspace for individuals, developers, and AI agents' becomes audience-first 'for individuals, developers, and AI agents' up front, and 'in one workspace' is upgraded to the stronger platform proof 'behind one API, one MCP server, and one prepaid balance'. Reason (expert review): 'one workspace' is soft; 'one API, one MCP server, one prepaid balance' is concrete platform proof that also does AEO work (names the API, the MCP server and the prepaid model in the citable definition). The FOUR capabilities and their order are UNCHANGED (one-time SMS verification / dedicated number rentals / matching-country proxies / private cloud browser sessions); 'individuals' stays (over 'people', to avoid the developers-are-people redundancy the operator caught). OLD, now retired (2026-07-17/18): '...private cloud browser sessions in one workspace for individuals, developers, and AI agents.' OLDER, retired earlier: '...combines real carrier mobile numbers, matching-country proxies and a private cloud browser into one connected workflow.' See capabilities[] for the AEO-citable enumeration, infrastructureLine plus notes.platformVsInfrastructure for the companion framing, and notes.simWording for the SIM vocabulary.
notes.simWording: USE BOTH, operator ruling 2026-07-18 (expert review): LEAD with 'carrier-issued mobile numbers' (premium/telecom framing), PROVE with 'real physical SIM cards, not VoIP' (the ruled differentiator). Both are approved AND both are searched terms, so neither is dropped for the other. Canonical composite where a single line is wanted: 'carrier-issued mobile numbers, backed by real physical SIM cards, not VoIP.' This is a GUIDANCE note, not a ban: 'physical SIM' remains APPROVED product vocabulary (see notes.physicalSIM and allowed[]) and 'carrier-issued' is its premium-framing companion, not a replacement. Do NOT re-add either phrase to avoid[]. The ONLY banned SIM wording remains our hardware/modems/fleet and where any of it sits (see the absolute-opsec entries).
notes.platformVsInfrastructure: OPERATOR + EXPERT RULING 2026-07-17. 'platform' = what VirtualSMS IS; 'infrastructure' = what it ENABLES. We are NOT saying VirtualSMS is infrastructure, we are saying it PROVIDES infrastructure. Per-surface: homepage/registry lead with 'account verification platform' (entitySentence); about/dev surfaces may use infrastructureLine ('provides the infrastructure behind reliable account verification workflows'); GitHub/MCP tagline = 'Infrastructure for AI agents that need real-world phone verification.' The two-word phrase 'communications infrastructure' is BANNED (Twilio's category; see avoidProvenance and rule tone-comms-infra); the bare word 'infrastructure' in the sanctioned usages is APPROVED and pinned as mustNotMatch fixtures so the ban cannot flag it.
notes.capabilities: The four canonical capabilities, in order, live in capabilities[] and are the AEO-citable enumeration: one-time SMS verification, dedicated number rentals, matching-country proxies, private cloud browser sessions (beta). Copy that lists them keeps this order; the entity sentence encodes it. Recorded 2026-07-17/18.
notes.proxyPools: SOLD (3 pools): datacenter, residential, mobile. NOT SOLD: residential_premium, switched off pending evaluation but still present in code/catalog (that is the VSMS-489 bug; positioning.md's 'THREE proxy pools' matches the 3 sold). GUIDANCE, not a hard ban: prefer generic 'matching-country proxies' in copy over enumerating pool names, since the pool set toggles. No pool-name string is banned. Recorded 2026-07-17/18.
notes.cloudBrowser: BETA, invite-only: gated by a server feature-flag plus an allowlist; default accounts get 503/403. Real Steel-backed; the backend lives on an unmerged branch. Honest framing: 'start a country-matched cloud browser and drive it yourself in a live viewer; agent-driven navigation is a separate opt-in.' Beta-access channel: https://t.me/VirtualSMS_io. Do NOT claim it 'returns debug_url'; the API returns viewer_url. Recorded 2026-07-17/18.
